title: Chocolate orange bark
subtitle: Tempered dark chocolate with candied orange peel and sea salt
excerpt: Dark chocolate melted and tempered to a glossy, snapping finish, then spread thin on baking parchment and topped with strips of candied orange peel, a pinch of flaked sea salt and toasted pistachios. Tempering gives the chocolate a crisp snap and the characteristic gloss of a properly finished bar; untempered chocolate blooms grey and has a softer, waxy texture. No oven is required. The process takes about 40 minutes plus 30 minutes setting time.
sourceNotes: Chocolate tempering is covered in standard confectionery texts including Paul Young's 'Adventures with Chocolate' (2012) and William Curley's 'Couture Chocolate' (2011). The tabling method for home tempering (melting most of the chocolate, reserving the rest to cool it) is the most practical version for small quantities without a marble slab. The bark format with flavoured toppings became popular in British confectionery from the 1990s onwards.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Bark keeps in an airtight tin in a cool, dry place (not the fridge) for up to 2 weeks. Refrigeration causes bloom.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: no oven required; tempering temperature for dark chocolate is 31 to 32 degrees C

glossaryTerms.definition: The process of melting and cooling chocolate in a controlled way to encourage the cocoa butter to form stable crystal structures (Form V crystals). Untempered chocolate sets with a dull, grey bloom and a soft, waxy texture because the cocoa butter is in an unstable crystalline form. Properly tempered chocolate sets hard and glossy with a clean snap. For the seeding method at home: melt three quarters of the chocolate to 50 degrees C, remove from the heat and add the remaining finely chopped chocolate, stirring constantly until the temperature drops to 31 to 32 degrees C for dark chocolate. Use it immediately at this temperature; it will set firm and glossy.

content.text: requires an accurate thermometer and patience rather than technique. Melt three quarters of the chocolate, cool to 31 to 32 degrees C by adding the reserved finely chopped chocolate, and use it immediately. If the chocolate cools below 28 degrees C in the bowl before you have poured it out, it will set thick and difficult to spread; rewarm gently to 31 degrees C and continue.

Use chocolate with at least 60 per cent cocoa solids. Lower-cocoa chocolate has more sugar and milk solids, which makes it harder to temper. Couverture chocolate (sold at specialist shops) has more cocoa butter and sets more reliably than standard bars.

items.prepNote: at least 60% cocoa solids, finely chopped and divided: 300 g to melt, 100 g reserved for seeding

content.text: Place the 300 g chopped chocolate in a heatproof bowl set over a pan of barely simmering water, making sure the bowl does not touch the water. Stir gently until fully melted and the temperature reaches 50 degrees C. Remove from the heat. Add the reserved 100 g finely chopped chocolate in three additions, stirring thoroughly between each. Continue stirring until the temperature drops to 31 to 32 degrees C; this takes 5 to 8 minutes. The chocolate is now tempered and should be used immediately.

Line a large baking tray with baking parchment. Pour the tempered chocolate onto the parchment and spread quickly with a palette knife or the back of a spoon to an even layer about 4 to 5 mm thick. Working quickly before the chocolate begins to set, scatter over the 60 g candied orange peel strips, 40 g toasted pistachios and 0.5 tsp flaked sea salt. Leave at room temperature in a cool, dry place for at least 30 minutes until fully set and hard. Break into irregular shards to serve.

items.problem: Chocolate set with a grey, streaky bloom
items.solution: The chocolate went out of temper before setting, either because it was poured below 29 degrees C or because the room was too warm. The chocolate is still safe to eat but lacks snap and gloss. For the next batch, use the chocolate promptly at 31 to 32 degrees C and set it in a cool room (below 20 degrees C).

items.problem: Chocolate seized into a thick, grainy paste during melting
items.solution: A drop of water got into the chocolate. Even a small amount of moisture causes the chocolate to seize. Ensure the bowl, spatula and any equipment are completely dry. If seized, it cannot be recovered for bark; use it in baking or ganache instead.

items.problem: Chocolate too thick to spread before it began to set
items.solution: The chocolate cooled below the working temperature. Set the bowl briefly back over the warm water, stirring to return it to 31 to 32 degrees C, then pour immediately.
